description: Extending the polymorphic comments table to support a new entity type. Review the polymorphic association pattern itself and evaluate whether the migration adequately addresses integrity.
review_checklist: Identify referential integrity gaps in polymorphic associations
Evaluate alternatives: separate comment tables, exclusion constraint, conditional FK
Check whether type values are constrained

ground_truth.description: Polymorphic association on comments(target_type, target_id) bypasses PostgreSQL's foreign key enforcement entirely. Nothing prevents target_id pointing to a deleted/non-existent post/photo/video/storymap. Application bugs or race conditions can leave orphan rows undetected forever. Recommended alternatives: (a) separate comments_on_post / comments_on_photo / comments_on_video tables with proper FKs, (b) one comments table with multiple nullable FK columns + a CHECK constraint that exactly one is set, (c) for advanced cases, an EXCLUDE constraint enforcing mutual exclusivity.

target_type column is unconstrained VARCHAR. Application code can write 'storymap', 'StoryMap', 'sm', 'story_map' inconsistently — exactly what the migration's UPDATE on line 11 is trying to fix. Use a CHECK constraint enumerating valid types or a small reference table; without it, future drift is guaranteed.

UPDATE comments SET target_type = 'storymap' WHERE target_type = 'sm' has no batching and no progress visibility. On a 200M-row comments table the UPDATE acquires row-level locks for every matched row in a single transaction, ballooning WAL volume and risking replication lag spikes. Batch in 5-10K row chunks with explicit commits.

New storymaps table missing critical indexing for common access patterns: no index on creator_id (queries 'storymaps by user' will seq-scan), no index on published (queries for public storymaps will seq-scan), no created_at for time-sorted feeds.

comments.target_id has no constraint on the type — a target_id of 12345 is just a bigint. If a post and a photo both have id=12345, application bugs can attribute comments to the wrong entity. With proper FK design (option b/c above), the type system enforces correctness.
